title: Writing - Part 2
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 140-190 words.

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt:
You have been asked to write a review for an English-language cultural website. Write a review of a museum, historical site, or heritage exhibition that you have visited. Describe what it offers, evaluate how well it presents its subject, and say whether you would recommend it to others.

Write your review.
tasks.modelAnswer:
The National Civil War Museum — History Brought to Life

For anyone with even a passing interest in history, the National Civil War Museum is an experience not to be missed. Housed in a beautifully restored Victorian building, the museum manages the remarkable feat of presenting a complex and often painful period of history with clarity, sensitivity, and genuine emotional power.

The permanent collection spans three floors and takes visitors chronologically through events, using an impressive combination of original artefacts, interactive displays, and first-person testimonies. I was particularly moved by the personal letters section, which gives voice to ordinary soldiers and civilians in a way that no textbook ever could.

The temporary photography exhibition currently on display is equally compelling, offering a rare glimpse into daily life during the conflict.

If I had one criticism, it would be that the café facilities are rather limited for such a popular attraction. Longer queues at weekends can also be frustrating.

Overall, however, this is an outstanding museum — thoughtfully curated, beautifully presented, and genuinely unforgettable. I would wholeheartedly recommend it to visitors of all ages.

tasks.title: Letter
tasks.prompt:
You have read a magazine article suggesting that old buildings should be demolished to make way for modern developments. Write a letter to the magazine editor disagreeing with this view, explaining why preserving local historical buildings is important, and suggesting how communities can protect their architectural heritage.

Write your letter to the editor.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Editor,

I read your recent article on urban regeneration with considerable interest, but I feel compelled to challenge the suggestion that demolishing historic buildings is a straightforward or desirable solution to our housing and development needs.

Historical buildings are not merely old structures — they are tangible connections to our shared past, embodying the craftsmanship, culture, and stories of previous generations. Once demolished, they are gone forever. No replica, however expertly built, can replicate the authentic character of an original building.

Moreover, heritage sites are significant economic assets. Studies consistently show that towns with well-preserved historic centres attract more tourists, support local businesses, and generate greater civic pride than those dominated by uniform modern developments.

Rather than choosing between preservation and progress, I would argue that the two can coexist. Sensitive renovation, adaptive reuse, and strong local heritage protection policies allow communities to modernise without erasing their identity.

I urge readers and local authorities alike to champion the preservation of our architectural heritage before it is irreversibly lost.

Yours sincerely,
A Heritage Advocate

tasks.title: Article
tasks.prompt:
You see this notice in an English-language magazine:

'History: What is the point? Some students find history boring and irrelevant to modern life. Others argue it is one of the most important subjects we can study. Write an article sharing your views on what we can learn from studying history.'

Write your article.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Those Who Cannot Remember the Past…

History has an image problem. To many students, it appears to be little more than a long list of dates, battles, and dead monarchs — dusty, irrelevant, and safely confined to the past. This perception, I would argue, is not only mistaken but potentially dangerous.

Studying history is, at its core, the study of human behaviour: how societies rise and fall, how power is gained and abused, how ordinary people respond to extraordinary circumstances. These patterns repeat themselves with remarkable regularity, and understanding them is one of the most practical skills a person can develop.

Consider how an understanding of past economic crises informs responses to modern ones, or how the study of historical propaganda helps us recognise misinformation today. History does not simply describe the past — it equips us to interpret the present.

Perhaps most importantly, history teaches empathy. When we read the accounts of people who lived through upheaval, suffering, or injustice, we develop a broader and more compassionate understanding of our shared humanity.

Far from being irrelevant, history may be the most urgently needed subject in modern education.
